nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated hill streams and mountain rivers with gravel, cobble, and boulder substrates covered in biofilm and diatom mats (aufwuchs); Red and Pearl River drainages and Hainan Island

decorPreferences: smooth flat rocks and river boulders for resting and aufwuchs grazing
cobble and pebble substrate allowing biofilm colonisation
powerful circulation pump or powerhead to simulate torrent current (10–15× turnover per hour)
mature tank with well-established diatom and biofilm growth on rock surfaces
low-profile decorations to preserve unobstructed flow lanes
fine sand or gravel interspersed between rocks for occasional substrate resting

diet.notes: Specialist aufwuchs grazer — scrapes biofilm, diatoms, and micro-algae from hard surfaces in the wild. In captivity, supplement the essential natural biofilm growth with spirulina wafers, algae tabs pressed to glass and rocks, blanched zucchini or cucumber, and high-quality sinking algae-based pellets. Will accept small frozen or live foods (daphnia, baby brine shrimp) as an occasional supplement. Plant-based items should constitute the bulk of the diet. Species can starve gradually in low-flow or recently set-up tanks where insufficient aufwuchs develops; heavy circulation and allowing algae to grow naturally on rocks are prerequisites.

goodWith: White Cloud Mountain Minnows (same cool-water preference)
other hillstream loaches (Sewellia, Gastromyzon — if same temperature range)
small cool-water danios (Zebra Danio, Celestial Pearl Danio)
dwarf shrimp (generally safe; monitor in small tanks)
avoidWith: tropical community fish requiring 26°C+ (Neon Tetras, Discus, Angelfish, Guppies — dangerously warm for this species)
large boisterous fish that outcompete for food at rock surfaces
fish requiring still or low-flow water
Beaufortia kweichowensis kept together without adequate space — intraspecific territorial posturing possible at feeding sites

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image URL pattern for Beaufortia leveretti (species id 55033); confirm URL resolves before use.
Approximate Wikimedia URL pattern; confirm file name and license before use.

summary: Leverett’s Hillstream Loach is a flattened, ray-like Beaufortia from fast-flowing rivers of southern China and Vietnam, closely related to the Butterfly Hillstream Loach (B. kweichowensis). Requires cool (18–24°C), heavily oxygenated, high-flow water and a mature tank with abundant biofilm and aufwuchs on rock surfaces; intermediate care due to strict flow, temperature, and diet needs.
careNotes: Beaufortia leveretti is a torrent-adapted hillstream loach from the Red and Pearl River drainages of southern China and Vietnam, including Hainan Island. It shares the same fundamental requirements as its sister species B. kweichowensis but is distinguished by distribution, slightly variable colouration (golden base with dark spots), and a larger potential adult size of up to 10 cm, though most aquarium specimens are sold and kept at 5–7 cm. The species is closely associated in the hobby with its congener and care guidance often applies equally to both. Temperature must be kept at 18–24°C; sustained exposure above 24–25°C depletes dissolved oxygen and causes gradual decline. The Aquarium Glaser source notes an ideal seasonal range of approximately 12°C in winter and 21°C in summer, making this species particularly suitable for unheated or lightly heated aquaria in temperate rooms. Strong water movement is non-negotiable: target 10–15× tank volume per hour using a powerful powerhead or circulation pump, directed across flat rock surfaces where the fish spend most of their time. A minimum of 80 L (75 cm footprint) is suitable for a small group, but wider tanks with greater rock surface area are strongly preferred. The most common cause of loss is starvation in tanks where aufwuchs (the thin diatom-biofilm layer on illuminated rock surfaces) has not developed. This species does not consume macroalgae sheets as its primary food; it requires the micro-layer that accumulates in well-lit, high-flow, mature conditions. Supplement generously with spirulina wafers, algae tabs fixed to rocks or glass, and blanched vegetables. Water quality must be pristine: low nitrates, high dissolved oxygen, and regular partial water changes are essential. Keep in groups of at least three; minor territorial interactions at feeding surfaces are normal and rarely escalate. The species name honours Rev. William J. Leverett, an American Presbyterian missionary on Hainan Island (1894–1924). The species was formally described by Nichols and Pope in 1927; synonym: Gastromyzon leveretti.
breedingNotes: Captive spawning is rarely reported. Males are believed to develop minor nuptial tubercles on the snout at breeding condition. Conditioning with a mild seasonal temperature dip (simulating winter–spring transition), increased current flow, a highly varied diet rich in live aufwuchs and spirulina, and a species-only hillstream biotope setup are the conditions most likely to trigger spawning. Eggs are deposited in crevices or under rocks in high-flow zones. Fry are tiny and require an abundant supply of micro-aufwuchs; cultured micro-algae or infusoria can supplement natural biofilm. Breeding success remains uncommon in the hobby.

primary.name: FishBase — Beaufortia leveretti
primary.notes: Primary source. Confirmed taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), maximum size (12.0 cm TL, common 5.0 cm SL), native range (China and Vietnam: Red and Pearl River systems, Hainan Island), habitat (mountain streams with gravel bottom, demersal), temperature range (18–24°C), trophic level 2.6 (herbivore-leaning), and conservation status (Least Concern, IUCN 2010). Species description: Nichols & Pope, 1927; synonym Gastromyzon leveretti.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Specialist hobbyist article dedicated to B. leveretti. Confirmed size (to 12 cm / 5 inches), temperature range (65–75°F / 18–24°C), water chemistry (moderately hard, neutral to slightly basic), dietary needs (plant-based bulk, sinking foods accepted), high-flow and oxygenation requirements, peaceful temperament, and limited captive breeding reports. Notes challenges with shipping stress on initial acquisition.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Hillstream Loach Week Part 2: Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Trade importer’s species profile. Confirmed origin (China and Vietnam, imported from China), distinctive golden body with black spots, ideal seasonal temperature range (12°C winter, 21°C summer), suitability for unheated aquaria, and pairing with White Cloud Mountain Minnows (15–20 specimens in water column with 10–12 Beaufortia). Valuable source on real-world temperature seasonality.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Beaufortia leveretti
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), maximum length (12 cm), native range (Red and Pearl River systems, China and Vietnam, Hainan Island), and conservation status (Least Concern, IUCN 2010). Confirmed synonym Gastromyzon leveretti Nichols & Pope, 1927.

additional.name: Aqualapp — Beaufortia leveretti care and compatibility
additional.notes: Hobby-oriented profile specific to B. leveretti. Confirmed size range (6–10 cm), temperature (18–24°C), pH (6.6–7.5), softish-water preference (gH 5–15), peaceful temperament with possible minor conspecific territorial behaviour at feeding sites, group-keeping recommendation (minimum 3), and herbivorous diet. Minimum tank suggestion of 15 L in source is too low for practical group husbandry; 80 L used as practical minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Conditioning requires seasonal temperature dip, increased current, and abundant live aufwuchs. Eggs deposited in rock crevices under high-flow conditions; fry require micro-aufwuchs. Species-only hillstream biotope setup improves success.
